eates unpredictability) and with philosophical ideas of free will vs determinism (showing determinism need not mean predictability). It also reveals fractal patterns (order within chaos) – many chaotic systems form fractals, linking to geometric self-similarity as a cross-domain motif.
(Mathematics and logic provide the rigorous backbone for many principles. We see recurring themes of self-reference (Gödel, Halting Problem, paradoxes), infinity and limits (induction, Zeno), and binary vs continuum (laws of logic vs fuzzy reality). These formal principles often double as metaphors for consistency, certainty, and limitation in other domains.)3. Philosophy (Ethics, Metaphysics, Epistemology, Logic)
• Occam’s Razor (Law of Parsimony) (Domain: Philosophy/Science) – A problem-solving principle credited to William of Ockham (~14th century) stating that one should not multiply entities beyond necessity. In plain terms: given multiple explanations for a phenomenon, the simplest explanation (the one requiring the fewest assumptions) should be preferred【44†L329-L335】. Examples: If you hear hoofbeats, think horses not unicorns – i.e. don’t assume a fantastical cause when a common one suffices. In science, Occam’s razor favors the theory that explains data with fewer adjustable parameters. For instance, in explaining planetary motion, Newton’s law of gravitation is simpler (one law for all) than invoking separate crystal spheres for each planet. Implications: Occam’s razor is a heuristic guiding us toward simplicity and elegance in theory. It’s not an iron-clad law (the simplest explanation can be wrong), but it underpins scientific modeling and rational inquiry. Philosophically, it connects to metaphysical parsimony – don’t assume more types of things than you need. It resonates with the aesthetic notion that truth often has simplicity. In a symbolic sense, it’s a call for eco
